title: The Blockade
subtitle: Put a piece in front of the pawn
summary: Turn a dangerous passed pawn into a target by occupying the square directly in front of it.
attribution: Original prose; ideas after Nimzowitsch, My System. No book text reproduced.

steps.title: Do not chase from behind
steps.markdown: A [[passed-pawn|passed pawn]] wants to run. The square directly in front of it is the [[blockade]] square. Occupy that square and the pawn cannot move.

steps.title: Find the blockade square
steps.markdown: Black's d-pawn is ready to march. Move the knight to the square that freezes it.

hints.text: A black pawn on d4 moves toward d3.
The knight can jump to the square directly in front of the pawn.

feedback.correct: Good. The knight blockades the pawn and cannot be pushed away by that pawn.
feedback.wrong: Put the knight on the square the pawn wants to move to.

steps.title: Why a knight?
steps.markdown: Nimzowitsch loved knights as blockaders. Why do they work so well?

options.label: They still attack from a fixed square
options.explain: Exactly. A knight can sit in front of the pawn and still jump into the enemy camp.

options.label: They can be pushed by pawns
options.explain: The pawn being blockaded cannot push the knight away because the knight is on its next square.

options.label: They move like rooks
options.explain: Knights jump, which is what makes them useful from a cramped blockade square.

steps.title: Stop, then attack
steps.markdown: First restrain the passed pawn. Then blockade it. Once it cannot move, the pawn often changes from a strength into a weakness your pieces can attack.
